items.title: Eyelid BCC: incidence
items.contentHtml:
eyelid BCC

  • eyelid basal cell carcinoma is the most common malignant eyelid tumor, surpassing squamous cell carcinoma and melanoma in a 10:1 ratio.
  • It represents 80-90% of all malignant neoplasms of the eyelid.
  • Its origin is from basal cells of the eyelid. epidermis and hair follicles.

items.title: UV and CBC radiation
items.contentHtml:
Chronic sun exposure

  • Chronic sun exposure to ultraviolet radiation (mainly UV-B) is the main etiological factor, causing mutations in suppressor genes such as PTCH1.
  • Cumulative actinic damage explains the higher incidence in phototypes I-II and in photoexposed areas such as the lower eyelid.
  • Sun protection and avoiding direct exposure reduce the risk.

items.foco: Most common location on the lower eyelid
items.title: Lower eyelid: predominant site
items.contentHtml:
Anatomical location

  • The most common location in the lower eyelid represents ~50% of cases, followed by the inner canthus (25%), upper eyelid (15%) and outer canthus (10%).
  • The predominance in the lower eyelid is explained by the greater direct sun exposure and less protection of the superciliary arch.

items.title: Nodular BCC: key findings
items.contentHtml:
Nodular subtype

  • The nodular subtype with telangiectasias is the most frequent (60-80%): pearly, shiny papule, with superficial telangiectasias and raised edges.
  • It usually presents central ulceration (“gnawing ulcer”) and slow growth.
  • Histologically it shows nodules of cells. basaloids in peripheral palisade.

items.title: Sclerodermiform BCC: surgical challenge
items.contentHtml:
Sclerodermiform subtype

  • The sclerodermiform subtype that is difficult to delimit is morpheaform, with a scarred appearance, poorly defined borders and subclinical extension greater than what is visible.
  • It requires Mohs micrographic surgery due to its high recurrence rate if margins are not ensured.
  • Histology: thin cords of basaloid cells immersed in dense fibrous stroma.

items.foco: Low metastatic potential but local invasion
items.title: Metastasis vs local invasion
items.contentHtml:
Metastatic potential

  • BCC has low metastatic potential but destructive local invasion; the metastasis rate is <0.1%.
  • Local invasion can affect orbit, paranasal sinuses and deep structures, causing significant morbidity.
  • Metastatic risk factors: size >3 cm, recurrence, perineural invasion and sclerodermiform subtype.

items.title: Margin control
items.contentHtml:
Surgical margins

  • Standard oncological resection requires surgical margins with histopathological control of 3-5 mm in nodular BCC and 5-10 mm in aggressive subtypes.
  • Intraoperative study of margins (freezing) reduces recurrences.
  • The recurrence rate with positive margins is >30%.

items.title: Mohs Indications
items.contentHtml:
Mohs micrographic surgery

  • The Mohs micrographic surgery is indicated in high-risk BCC: recurrent, sclerodermiform, poorly defined borders, location in the inner canthus or eyelid, diameter >2 cm and perineural invasion.
  • It allows the evaluation of 100% of the margins with maximum tissue conservation.
  • Healing rate >99% in expert hands.

items.title: Post-surgical recurrence
items.contentHtml:
Recurrence

  • The recurrence if incomplete resection is 30-50% at 5 years, especially in sclerodermiform and infiltrative subtypes.
  • Clinical follow-up should be every 6-12 months to detect early recurrences.
  • Adjuvant radiotherapy is considered in cases of non-reoperable positive margins.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: seborrheic keratosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis

  • The differential diagnosis with seborrheic keratosis is key: seborrheic keratosis has a warty, “stuck” appearance, with horny plugs and without telangiectasias or ulceration.
  • Other Differential diagnosis: squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, intradermal nevus, melanoma amelanotic.
  • Dermatoscopy helps: CBC presents arboriform vessels, while seborrheic keratosis shows milio pseudocysts and fissures.

items.foco: Risk factors: light skin and advanced age
items.title: Classic risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • The risk factors: light skin and advanced age are the most consistent; phototypes I-II and >50 years multiply the risk.
  • Others: chronic sun exposure, immunosuppression, previous radiotherapy, exposure to arsenic and genetic syndromes.

items.foco: Gorlin syndrome associated with multiple BCCs
items.title: Gorlin syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Gorlin syndrome

  • Gorlin syndrome associated with multiple BCCs (basal cell nevus syndrome) is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner by mutation in PTCH1.
  • Major criteria: >2 BCCs or one <20 years, odontogenic keratocysts, calcification of the falx cerebri, palms/soles with pits.
  • Requires monitoring from childhood and avoiding radiotherapy due to the risk of new BCC.
